items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).

items.q: Why do tertiary halogenoalkanes typically react via SN1 rather than SN2?
items.correct: The tertiary carbocation intermediate is stabilised by three alkyl groups (positive inductive effect), making the SN1 pathway favourable.
items.misconception: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN1 because they are more reactive in every way.
items.random_wrong: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN2 because they have more bonds to break.
items.why: Students mix up steric and electronic factors — they don't realise it's the STABILITY of the carbocation (electronic) plus STERIC HINDRANCE (blocking SN2) that drives the SN1 preference.

items.q: In 1H NMR, what does the n+1 rule predict?
items.correct: A proton with n equivalent neighbouring protons will appear as a peak split into n+1 sub-peaks.
items.misconception: Splitting tells you how many protons are in the same chemical environment as the one giving the peak.
items.random_wrong: The number of peaks equals the total number of protons in the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse splitting (about NEIGHBOURING protons) with the number of equivalent protons producing the peak (which affects peak area/integration, not splitting).
It's CN⁻. “H⁺” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Members of a homologous series share:
items.correct: The same functional group and general formula
items.why: It's The same functional group and general formula. “The same molecular mass” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
